about: Short starter outlines for practice only. Official AP course and exam descriptions are published by the College Board at apstudents.collegeboard.org.

topics.title: APUSH — Period 1 (1491–1607)
topics.content:
Native North American societies before sustained European contact.

Key ideas: diverse cultures, trade networks, agriculture, and political structures adapted to geography. European exploration begins to redraw maps and trade routes.

Study focus: compare regions (e.g., Southwest vs. Northeast). Know one example of long-distance exchange before Columbus.

Common mistakes: treating all Indigenous nations as one culture; forgetting environmental context.

Exam tip: tie evidence to historical thinking skills—causation, continuity, and change over time.

topics.title: APUSH — Period 3 (1754–1800)
topics.content:
Imperial conflict, independence, and building new governments.

Key ideas: French and Indian War aftermath, taxes and protest, Declaration and war, Articles vs. Constitution, first party debates.

Study focus: explain how debates over sovereignty and rights shaped founding documents.

Common mistakes: conflating colonial grievances with later Civil War issues.

Exam tip: practice thesis statements that use specific evidence from two different colonies or regions.

topics.title: APUSH — Period 4 (1800–1848)
topics.content:
Expansion, market revolution, and reform.

Key ideas: territorial growth, transportation, labor systems, sectional tensions, abolition and women’s rights movements.

Study focus: connect economic change to social and political consequences.

Common mistakes: oversimplifying “North vs. South” before 1850.

Exam tip: use one reform movement as a case study for continuity vs. change.

topics.title: APUSH — Period 7 (1890–1945)
topics.content:
Industrial America, empire, Progressivism, world wars, and New Deal.

Key ideas: immigration, labor unions, Populism/Progressivism, WWI home front, Great Depression responses, WWII mobilization.

Study focus: compare Progressive goals with outcomes; link foreign policy shifts to domestic debates.

Common mistakes: listing programs without explaining purpose or constraint.

Exam tip: LEQ practice—evaluate continuity in federal power across two eras.

topics.title: AP World — Renaissance & global encounters
topics.content:
Intellectual and artistic renewal in Europe alongside expanding maritime exploration.

Key ideas: humanism, printing, patronage; Iberian navigation; early Atlantic exchanges.

Study focus: connect cultural change to economic and political elites.

Common mistakes: treating Renaissance as purely “rebirth of Greece/Rome” with no social context.

Exam tip: compare one European development with a contemporaneous Asian state-building example.

topics.title: AP World — Industrialization & imperialism
topics.content:
Steam power, factories, and global competition reshape labor, trade, and empire.

Key ideas: demographic shifts, reform responses, technologies of rule, resistance.

Study focus: link industrial demand to colonial extraction and infrastructure.

Common mistakes: assuming imperialism was only “European vs. everyone else.”

Exam tip: causation chain—one resource, one technology, one political outcome.

topics.title: AP World — 1914–present (war, peace, globalization)
topics.content:
World wars, decolonization, Cold War, and institutions of global interdependence.

Key ideas: self-determination, superpower rivalry, international organizations, migration, human rights language.

Study focus: compare post-1945 economic orders in two regions.

Common mistakes: Cold War as only nuclear crisis—include economics and proxy conflicts.

Exam tip: document-based reasoning—group sources by perspective before thesis.

topics.title: AP Gov — Constitutional principles
topics.content:
Founding debates, separation of powers, checks and balances, federalism.

Key ideas: enumerated vs. reserved powers; elastic clause debates; Bill of Rights as limits on national power.

Study focus: apply a scenario: which branch checks which, and why?

Common mistakes: mixing up Articles of Confederation problems with later federalism disputes.

Exam tip: define terms precisely (e.g., devolution, mandate, commerce power).

topics.title: AP Gov — Civil liberties & civil rights
topics.content:
Bill of Rights incorporation, selective incorporation, equal protection, landmark cases.

Key ideas: speech/religion/assembly limits; due process; disparate impact vs. intent.

Study focus: compare two amendments used in similar fact patterns.

Common mistakes: using “civil liberties” and “civil rights” interchangeably.

Exam tip: SCOTUS reasoning—majority concern vs. dissent concern in one case.

topics.title: AP Gov — Elections, parties, & media
topics.content:
Linkage institutions, nomination processes, campaign finance, polarization, news consumption.

Key ideas: primaries vs. caucuses; PACs/Super PACs; agenda setting and framing.

Study focus: explain how a rule change shifts who wins nominations.

Common mistakes: claiming parties are identical; ignoring state-level variation.

Exam tip: free-response—use a real mechanism (e.g., electoral college) not vague “people vote.”

topics.title: AP Bio — Chemistry of life & cells
topics.content:
Water properties, carbon chemistry, macromolecules, enzyme basics, membrane structure and transport.

Key ideas: structure–function; competitive vs. noncompetitive inhibition; passive vs. active transport.

Study focus: explain how surface area/volume ratio matters for cell size.

Common mistakes: confusing hypertonic/hypotonic direction of water movement.

Exam tip: draw quick sketches for osmosis in animal vs. plant cells.

topics.title: AP Bio — Cell energetics
topics.content:
Photosynthesis and cellular respiration as coupled pathways of energy flow.

Key ideas: ATP, redox, glycolysis, Krebs, ETC; light reactions vs. Calvin cycle.

Study focus: track carbons and electron carriers at a big-picture level.

Common mistakes: saying “plants only do photosynthesis” without noting they respire too.

Exam tip: compare aerobic vs. anaerobic outcomes in muscle vs. yeast contexts.

topics.title: AP Bio — Heredity & gene expression
topics.content:
Mendelian patterns, extensions, linkage, DNA replication, transcription, translation, regulation.

Key ideas: probability with Punnett squares; central dogma; operons as simple regulation models.

Study focus: predict phenotypes with incomplete dominance or codominance.

Common mistakes: mixing replication enzymes with transcription enzymes.

Exam tip: Chi-square vocabulary—null hypothesis, degrees of freedom, “fail to reject.”

topics.title: AP Micro — Supply, demand, & elasticity
topics.content:
Law of demand, law of supply, equilibrium, consumer/producer surplus, price controls.

Key ideas: determinants of demand vs. quantity demanded; elasticity along a curve vs. shifts.

Study focus: show deadweight loss from a binding price ceiling.

Common mistakes: drawing supply/demand shifts without explaining which curve moves and why.

Exam tip: label axes and equilibrium clearly before writing paragraphs.

topics.title: AP Micro — Market structures
topics.content:
Perfect competition, monopoly, monopolistic competition, oligopoly.

Key ideas: MR=MC rule; barriers to entry; game theory intuition for oligopoly.

Study focus: compare long-run profits across structures.

Common mistakes: calling any firm with branding a monopoly.

Exam tip: use a table—# firms, product type, entry, long-run profit.

topics.title: AP Micro — Externalities & public goods
topics.content:
When private markets miss social costs or benefits, policy tools try to realign incentives.

Key ideas: negative vs. positive externalities; MSC/MPC curves; Pigouvian taxes/subsidies; non-rivalry and non-excludability.

Study focus: show deadweight loss from overproduction of a good with a negative externality.

Common mistakes: confusing external costs with “bad taste” or low demand.

Exam tip: label private vs. social curves and the efficient quantity on one diagram.

topics.title: APHG — Thinking geographically
topics.content:
Maps, scales, projections, diffusion, and how geographers ask “why there?”

Key ideas: absolute vs. relative location; formal/functional/perceptual regions; distance-decay and time-space compression.

Study focus: compare two types of map distortion for the same world region.

Common mistakes: treating GPS dots as the same thing as cultural regions.

Exam tip: use one real-world example of relocation diffusion vs. expansion diffusion.

topics.title: APHG — Population & migration
topics.content:
Demographic transition, push/pull factors, refugee patterns, policies at borders.

Key ideas: crude rates, dependency ratio, guest workers, brain drain/brain gain.

Study focus: explain one policy that raises or lowers labor inflows.

Common mistakes: assuming all migration is permanent or voluntary.

Exam tip: chain reasoning—economic change → incentive → migration stream.

topics.title: APHG — Cities & urban land use
topics.content:
Models of urban structure, bid rent, suburbanization, and challenges of rapid growth.

Key ideas: CBD, sector vs. multiple nuclei; squatter settlements; gentrification debates.

Study focus: apply bid-rent logic to why retail vs. housing ring the core differently.

Common mistakes: forcing every real city to fit one classic ring model.

Exam tip: pair a model prediction with one counterexample from a named world city.

topics.title: AP Psych — Research methods & ethics
topics.content:
How psychologists design studies, measure variables, and protect participants.

Key ideas: IV/DV, operational definitions, random assignment, confounds, informed consent/debriefing.

Study focus: decide whether a headline supports causation or only correlation.

Common mistakes: saying “prove” from a single survey.

Exam tip: name one threat to internal validity and how randomization reduces it.

topics.title: AP Psych — Biological bases of behavior
topics.content:
Neurons, neurotransmitters, brain regions, endocrine system, genetics.

Key ideas: action potential basics; agonists vs. antagonists; plasticity after injury or practice.

Study focus: map one behavior to two levels of analysis (e.g., neural + cognitive).

Common mistakes: localizing complex traits to a single “spot” in the brain.

Exam tip: use split-brain or hemispheric examples only with correct task wording.

topics.title: AP Psych — Learning & memory
topics.content:
Classical and operant conditioning, observational learning, encoding/storage/retrieval.

Key ideas: acquisition/extinction/spontaneous recovery; schedules of reinforcement; shallow vs. deep processing.

Study focus: label UCS/UCR/CS/CR in a novel scenario.

Common mistakes: mixing negative reinforcement with punishment.

Exam tip: define terms in your own words before applying them to a vignette.

topics.title: AP Precalc — Functions & transformations
topics.content:
Parent functions, shifts, stretches, compositions, and inverse ideas.

Key ideas: domain/range after transformations; even/odd symmetry; one-to-one tests for inverses.

Study focus: write the equation of a transformed graph from a description.

Common mistakes: horizontal shifts with opposite sign confusion.

Exam tip: check inverses by composition f(f⁻¹(x)) on paper for one simple case.

topics.title: AP Precalc — Trigonometry & models
topics.content:
Unit circle, radians, graphs of sine/cosine, identities at an introductory level, simple harmonic motion.

Key ideas: period, amplitude, phase shift; solving triangles with law of sines/cosines.

Study focus: build a sinusoidal model from amplitude, midline, and period data.

Common mistakes: mixing degrees and radians in the same equation.

Exam tip: draw a quick unit-circle diagram before evaluating special angles.

topics.title: AP Precalc — Sequences & exponential change
topics.content:
Arithmetic vs. geometric sequences, partial sums, discrete vs. continuous growth models.

Key ideas: common difference/ratio; explicit vs. recursive forms; linking exponentials to percent change.

Study focus: convert a word problem into a sequence or exponential model.

Common mistakes: using linear growth when the problem states repeated percent increases.

Exam tip: state whether a model is discrete (counts steps) or continuous (time flowing).

topics.title: AP Chem — Atomic structure & periodicity
topics.content:
Light and matter, quantization, electron configuration, periodic trends.

Key ideas: ionization energy, atomic radius, electronegativity patterns; exceptions you can explain.

Study focus: compare two elements in the same period for size vs. IE.

Common mistakes: thinking higher IE always means “more reactive metals.”

Exam tip: tie trend direction to effective nuclear charge and shell shielding in one sentence.

topics.title: AP Chem — Stoichiometry & solutions
topics.content:
Moles, limiting reactants, titration logic, concentration units, dilutions.

Key ideas: mole roadmaps; M₁V₁ = M₂V₂ for dilution; percent yield vs. theoretical yield.

Study focus: identify the limiting reactant without arithmetic errors.

Common mistakes: confusing molarity with molality in lab contexts.

Exam tip: carry units through every line to catch flipped ratios early.

topics.title: AP Stats — Exploring one-variable data
topics.content:
Distributions, center, spread, unusual values, and graphs that tell a truthful story.

Key ideas: mean vs. median resistance; IQR and outliers; skew vs. symmetry.

Study focus: justify which measure of center matches a skewed salary distribution.

Common mistakes: calling any high value an “outlier” without a rule.

Exam tip: link graph shape to choice of summary statistics in one short paragraph.

topics.title: AP Stats — Designing studies & inference basics
topics.content:
Sampling methods, experiments vs. observational studies, randomization, and the logic of confidence intervals and tests (intro).

Key ideas: bias sources; blocking; placebo controls; scope of inference to populations/causes.

Study focus: explain why random assignment supports causation claims.

Common mistakes: generalizing beyond the sampled population.

Exam tip: name the study type before you interpret any p-value or interval.

topics.title: AP Calc AB — Limits & continuity
topics.content:
Limit intuition, one-sided limits, continuity conditions, removable discontinuities.

Key ideas: limit laws; sandwich theorem at a high level; continuity on an interval.

Study focus: decide continuity at a piecewise junction using left/right limits.

Common mistakes: substituting into expressions that are undefined at the point.

Exam tip: show limit work even when the answer “looks obvious” from a graph.

topics.title: AP Calc AB — Derivatives as rates of change
topics.content:
Derivative definition, basic rules, and interpreting f′ in context.

Key ideas: power rule; product/quotient; units of a derivative; marginal cost/revenue intuition.

Study focus: compute a derivative and interpret the sign in a word problem.

Common mistakes: dropping inner factors in simple chain-rule cases.

Exam tip: when in doubt, write “units of derivative = units of output per unit of input.”
